A surge of warm, humid, unstable air into southern WI resulted in scattered thunderstorms over the area. One storm produced severe hail.

Wind chills of 35 below or colder occurred across portions of central Wisconsin on the morning of January 7th. The dangerous wind chills occurred behind an area of low pressure that moved across the central Mississippi River Valley on January 6th and as a strong area of high pressure dropped south out of Canada onto the High Plains. These two features combined to push air temperatures down into the single digits and teens below zero with strong northwest winds. The lowest recorded wind chill was 38 below from the automated weather observing equipment at the Medford (Taylor County) airport.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chills of 35 below or colder occurred across Taylor County on the morning of January 7th. The lowest recorded wind chill was 38 below from the automated weather observing equipment at the Medford airport.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree snapped at its base.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system brought heavy rains to western Wisconsin in the middle of December. Temperatures with this system remained above freezing allowing all the precipitation to fall as rain. Amounts ranged from around 2 inches to over 6 inches from Jackson County south to Grant County. The highest reported rainfall total in this portion of western Wisconsin was 6.27 inches near Viroqua (Vernon County). This rain caused in bank rises to occur on most rivers and streams along with minor river flooding along the Black, Yellow, Wisconsin and Kickapoo Rivers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from heavy rain pushed the Black River out of its banks near Galesville. The river crested at 13.38 feet, which was a little over a foot above the flood stage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A small central Wisconsin wildfire destroyed six buildings southeast of Necedah (Juneau County) on May 2nd. Dry conditions across central Wisconsin helped to fuel the fire that started on a warm afternoon. The fire destroyed three houses and three outbuildings. None of the houses were occupied and were only used for storage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three unoccupied homes and three outbuildings were destroyed by a small wildfire southeast of Necedah. The fire was fueled by dry conditions and started on a warm  afternoon. The fire burned about 4 acres.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with very high rainfall rates and heavy rain moved across western Wisconsin during the afternoon hours of June 15th. The rainfall resulted in a mudslide south of Bangor (La Crosse County) that blocked both lanes of traffic on State Highway 162. The rain also caused Dutch Creek to come out of its banks and take out a snowmobile trail bridge and a pedestrian bridge in Bangor. The high rainfall rates overcame the drainage system on the south side of the city of La Crosse (La Crosse County) resulting in street flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall rates overcame the drainage system on the south side of the city of La Crosse causing street flooding to occur along U.S. Highways 14/61.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered Thunderstorms developed and moved across southern WI during the afternoon and evening hours as a trough of low pressure approached the region. One strong thunderstorm produced a damaging wet microburst in Princeton.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: About eight trees, mostly rotted, were uprooted in Princeton from a wet microburst. Large branches were also down throughout the city. Minor damage to a home and a garage when a tree fell on them.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving line of thunderstorms associated with a trough of low pressure triggered very heavy rain in Baraboo and West Baraboo. Street flooding occurred throughout the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall of 2 to 2.25 inches in approximately one hour resulted in urban flooding in Baraboo and West Baraboo. The flooding problems were more focused in West Baraboo within construction zones that had temporary culverts in place. Some street closures were needed in these areas, which included South Boulevard near the Army National Guard, Casey's General Store, and at the roundabout near Campbell Park. Knee deep water and a few stalled cars were reported. There was some flooding in the restaurants of Culver's, Taco Bell, and resident basements.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface low pressure system and an upper level disturbance moving across Wisconsin combined to produce showers and scattered thunderstorms across most of the state.  One of the storms caused wind damage in southwest Waupaca County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed about two dozen trees, some more than a foot in diameter, near the corner of Holmnlane and Stratton Lake Roads, in southwest Waupaca County.  Most of the fallen trees were uprooted in the very wet, sandy soil.  Fallen trees damaged a garage and caused roof damage to four cottages.  The high winds also flattened nearby wheat fields.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of showers and thunderstorms developed in warm and humid air as a cold front moved into Wisconsin from the north. Many of the storms dropped pea to small marble size hail. Hail as large as penny size fell near Crandon (Forest Co.) and at Caldron Falls Reservoir (Marinette Co.).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms dropped penny size hail as they moved through the Crandon area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Large hail fell over portions of western Wisconsin during the late afternoon and evening of June 29th. The storms started dropping hail along the Buffalo/Trempealeau County line west of Independence while moving south. The storms then exited into Minnesota before coming back into Wisconsin near Prairie du Chien (Crawford County) and then continued into Grant County. The largest hail reported was golf ball sized in Arcadia and Centerville (Trempealeau County). The hail damaged about 30 percent of the apple crop at one orchard near Centerville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system moved across the Upper Mississippi River Valley and Great Lakes region. A large area of showers and thunderstorms moved across southern WI and produced 3 tornadoes in southeast WI.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF1 tornado caused sporadic damage along its path. A metal building system had approximately 40 percent of its roof tore away. There was minor damage to the roofs of two residential homes. Five wooden power poles were knocked down. Many tree branches were snapped with some whole trees snapped.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An early morning squall line moved across southwest Wisconsin on June 22nd. This squall line produced winds of 60 to 70 mph and two small tornadoes. The first tornado damaged trees along its path north of Wauzeka (Crawford County). The second tornado damaged a few homes and numerous trees along its path north of Westport and Port Andrews (Crawford & Richland Counties). As the squall line moved across southwest Wisconsin it produced wind damage across the southern sections of Crawford and Richland Counties and over much of Grant County. Trees, power lines and at least one barn were blown down near Casseville (Grant County), multiple power lines were snapped east of Prairie du Chien (Crawford County), barns were damaged north of Port Andrew (Richland County) and 65 to 70 mph winds with damage were reported in Hazel Green and Platteville (Grant County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown onto a house west of Platteville creating holes in the roof.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An early morning squall line moved across southwest Wisconsin on June 22nd. This squall line produced winds of 60 to 70 mph and two small tornadoes. The first tornado damaged trees along its path north of Wauzeka (Crawford County). The second tornado damaged a few homes and numerous trees along its path north of Westport and Port Andrews (Crawford & Richland Counties). As the squall line moved across southwest Wisconsin it produced wind damage across the southern sections of Crawford and Richland Counties and over much of Grant County. Trees, power lines and at least one barn were blown down near Casseville (Grant County), multiple power lines were snapped east of Prairie du Chien (Crawford County), barns were damaged north of Port Andrew (Richland County) and 65 to 70 mph winds with damage were reported in Hazel Green and Platteville (Grant County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A small tornado damaged trees along its path in southern Crawford County between Wauzeka and Steuben.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system moved across the Upper Mississippi River Valley and Great Lakes region. A large area of showers and thunderstorms moved across southern WI and produced 3 tornadoes in southeast WI.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF1 tornado caused a sporadic path of damage. The most severe damage was significant roof damage to 4 buildings that were warehouse buildings and metal building systems. One of those buildings sustained a collapsed wall. There was minor damage to a mobile home and a barn. A couple large retail signs were destroyed. There were many trees uprooted and snapped.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed during the afternoon of Wednesday, June 17th from the Twin Cities, south to the Iowa border. The storms near the Twin Cities drifted eastward into west central Wisconsin. Two weak tornadoes (EF-0) developed from a storm that moved across Polk and Dunn counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A weak tornado touched down briefly, damaging several trees, a small lightweight greenhouse that was covered in plastic, and  removed a few shingles from two outbuildings. The tornado moved into a field and dissipated. Winds were estimated at about 65 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large area of arctic high pressure built across the Northern Plains and Upper Midwest Sunday, and early Monday, January 5th. Temperatures by Sunday evening fell into the single digits and teens below zero, and with wind speeds around 15 mph, created wind chill values between -30 to -40, especially between Cumberland and Ladysmith.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Cumberland airport recorded recorded temperatures around -13 to -18 degrees during the late evening and overnight hours. Wind speeds held around 10 mph, with occasional gusts near 20 mph, which created wind chill values between -30 to -40 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An early morning squall line moved across southwest Wisconsin on June 22nd. This squall line produced winds of 60 to 70 mph and two small tornadoes. The first tornado damaged trees along its path north of Wauzeka (Crawford County). The second tornado damaged a few homes and numerous trees along its path north of Westport and Port Andrews (Crawford & Richland Counties). As the squall line moved across southwest Wisconsin it produced wind damage across the southern sections of Crawford and Richland Counties and over much of Grant County. Trees, power lines and at least one barn were blown down near Casseville (Grant County), multiple power lines were snapped east of Prairie du Chien (Crawford County), barns were damaged north of Port Andrew (Richland County) and 65 to 70 mph winds with damage were reported in Hazel Green and Platteville (Grant County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An estimated wind gust of 65 mph blew down trees and powerlines in and around Hazel Green. The winds also damaged farm buildings and homes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A narrow band of moderate to heavy snowfall affected areas south and west of Madison. This was a prolonged snow event that began by early afternoon on March 22nd and ended the morning of March 23rd. A shortwave trough and a strong frontal boundary in the middle levels of the atmosphere was responsible for the heavy snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two to ten inches of snowfall with the heaviest totals over the northeast portion of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Parts of southwest Wisconsin received heavy snow on the last day of January. The area was set up to for heavy snow after a cold front moved through the region on the 30th. This front was not very far south of the region as an area of low pressure moved along it, throwing moisture from the Gulf of Mexico into the cold air. As a result, heavy snow started falling during the evening of the 31st. This heavy snow continued into February 1st with two day totals of 6 to 10 inches common. The highest reported total was 10.5 inches near Eastman (Crawford County). In addition to the snow, the area of low pressure also produced winds of 20 to 40 mph that caused considerable drifting to occur.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: COOP and volunteer snow observers reported heavy snow across Crawford County during the evening of January 31st. The heavy snow continued into February 1st.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system from the Plains moved across the Ohio Valley.  More than a foot of snow fell in far southeast Wisconsin and northern Illinois, and more than 6 inches fell as far north as Manitowoc.  The highest reported snowfall totals were 7.2 inches at Two Rivers, and 7.0 inches at Francis Creek.  Strong winds developed during the storm, gusting over 30 mph at times, causing blowing and drifting of the new snow.  Visibility dropped to near zero at the height of the storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The highest reported snowfall total from the storm was 7.2 inches at Two Rivers.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Temperatures in the 12 below to 22 below zero range combined with west winds of 10 to 20 mph to produce dangerous wind chills during the night of January 4th and the morning of the 5th.  These dangerous wind chills were mainly across parts of central and north central Wisconsin.  The wind chill dropped as low as 44 below zero at Rhinelander (Oneida Co.).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The coldest wind chill recorded in Vilas County was 39 below zero at Manitowish Waters.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The death of a 2 year old child occurred when she spent the night outdoors of an apartment in Tomah (Monroe County). Temperatures in the Tomah area are believed to have been in the single digits above zero for most of the early morning hours of February 17th. Winds were generally around 5 mph, but when combined with the air temperatures, produced wind chills of 5 to 10 below zero. Exposure to these conditions is believed to have contributed to the child's death.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 2 year old child died after spending the night outside of an apartment in Tomah when temperatures fell into the single digits above zero with wind chills of 5 to 10 below zero.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The coldest wind chill recorded in Marathon County was 36 below zero at Mosinee.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Wind chills of 35 below or colder occurred across central Wisconsin on the morning of January 5th. These dangerous wind chills occurred as air temperatures plummeted into the teens and 20s below zero with northwest winds of 5 to 10 mph behind a cold front. The lowest recorded wind chill was 38 below from the automated weather observing equipment at the Black River Falls (Jackson County) airport.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chills of 35 below or colder occurred across Taylor County on the morning of January 5th. The lowest recorded wind chill was 36 below from the automated weather observing equipment at the Medford airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Wind chills of 35 below or colder occurred across portions of central Wisconsin on the morning of January 7th. The dangerous wind chills occurred behind an area of low pressure that moved across the central Mississippi River Valley on January 6th and as a strong area of high pressure dropped south out of Canada onto the High Plains. These two features combined to push air temperatures down into the single digits and teens below zero with strong northwest winds. The lowest recorded wind chill was 38 below from the automated weather observing equipment at the Medford (Taylor County) airport.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chills of 35 below or colder occurred across Clark County on the morning of January 7th. The lowest recorded wind chill was 37 below from a mesonet reporting station near Loyal.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system moved southeast from the Dakotas into the central Mississippi River Valley from March 22nd into the 23rd. Heavy snow fell north of this system, with portions of western Wisconsin receiving between 6 and 15 inches. The snow fell at rates of 2 inches an hour at times, resulting in quick accumulations. Several accidents occurred as the roads quickly became snow covered. Numerous schools were either closed or had delayed starts on the 23rd. The highest reported snowfall total was 15 inches in Woodman (Grant County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: COOP and volunteer snow observers reported 5 to 8 inches of snow across La Crosse County. The highest reported total was 8.5 inches near Holmen.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: COOP and volunteer snow observers reported 6 to 7 inches of snow across Richland County. The highest reported total was 7.5 inches near Boaz.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The passing of a weak area of low pressure resulted in areas of freezing drizzle and light snow over portions of south central and southeast WI. There were some slide-offs and accidents,  including an indirect fatality in Columbia County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An icy roadway from freezing drizzle led to a fatal car accident around 5:45 am in the Town of Leeds. The man was killed when his car slid into an oncoming pickup truck.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Temperatures of 15 to 25 degrees below zero and  5 to 10 mph winds resulted in wind chills near 40 degrees below zero.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: One person was killed in a two vehicle crash that occurred on icy roads south of Dorchester (Clark County). Light freezing rain fell for a short period during the morning of March 29th and created the icy roads. The driver that was killed, lost control and spun into the path of an oncoming vehicle. Three other people sustained non-live threatening injuries in the crash.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One person was  killed in a two vehicle accident that occurred on icy roads south of Dorchester. Three other people sustained non-live threatening injuries in the crash.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Freezing drizzle resulted in a light coating of ice on roadways, mainly in southern Dodge County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another arctic cold front moved south across the Upper Midwest Tuesday, January 6, and dropped wind chill values to around -35 degrees below zero by Wednesday morning, January 7. Numerous schools closed or were delayed a few hours due to dangerous cold wind chills that continued through the late morning. Although the winds continued to gust in the afternoon, temperatures rebounded into the single digits above zero, and lessened the overall dangerous wind chill values.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Local weather stations recorded wind speeds around 10 to 15 mph, with occasional gusts in excess of 25 mph during the morning of Wednesday, January 7th. As temperatures fell into the teens below zero, wind chill values fell to -35 degrees and continued for a few hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Intensifying low pressure tracked from the central Great Plains to southeast Indiana the night of January 31st into the evening of February 1st. This resulted in a long duration winter storm and blizzard over portions of southern WI. Snowfall of 6 to 14 inches accumulated over far southern and eastern WI. Winds gusted from 30 to 40 mph with blizzard conditions in Racine and Kenosha Counties including near Lake MI over Milwaukee County. Vehicle slide-offs and accidents were prevalent. The Milwaukee County Medical Examiner Office reported the death of three men who died after collapsing from shoveling snow. The men had a history of heart disease.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ten to twelve inches of snow accumulation. Frequent whiteout conditions from heavy snow and blowing snow, including northeast wind gusts of 35-40 mph.  The blizzard conditions were more prevalent over the eastern half of the county due to the strong onshore winds from Lake MI.

Several accidents occurred as the roads quickly became snow covered. Numerous schools were either closed or had delayed starts on the 23rd. The highest reported snowfall total was 15 inches in Woodman (Grant County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: COOP and volunteer snow observers reported 8 to 13 inches of snow across Crawford County. The highest reported total was 13 inches in Prairie du Chien.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: COOP and volunteer snow observers reported 3 to 15 inches of snow across Grant County. All except the far western and southern parts of the county received amounts of 6 inches or more. The highest reported total was 15 inches in Woodman and Fennimore.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The approach of a warm front brought numerous showers and thunderstorms, and heavy rain to southern WI the night of April 8th into the morning of April 9th. Low pressure then tracked along the warm front that was located along the WI and IL border in the evening. Numerous thunderstorms developed over southern WI and northern IL from the late afternoon and evening hours. Several of the thunderstorms produced severe hail in southern WI.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 100 yard stretch of southbound I-43 became flooded resulting in two vehicles mostly submerged in standing water. The flooding was under the overpass and a clogged drain significantly contributed to the flooding. Approximately 2-3 inches of rain had fallen during the overnight period.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Milwaukee County Medical Examiner's office reported the death of a homeless man from hypothermia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A homeless man was found deceased in an alcove near a liquor store. The Milwaukee County Medical Examiner's office determined hypothermia as the cause of death. Intoxication was another significant condition in his death. Temperatures ranged from 15-30 degrees throughout the calendar day.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of high based showers and its gust front interacted with the lake breeze front resulting in a gustnado. The gustnado damaged the roof of a barn.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A gustnado formed and removed about 25% of the roof of a barn. The debris was blown about 0.25-0.50 miles downwind of the barn. Witnesses saw rotating debris in the air with little to no rotation at cloud base.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm that moved across the northern and eastern parts of the Twin Cities, moved across west central Wisconsin between Hudson and Baldwin and produced large hail and damaging winds. Hail depth was measured up to one foot near Hudson, Wisconsin, with several large trees blown down near Baldwin.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A flag pole and an eight inch diameter tree were blown down east of Hudson.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A lightning strike from an early morning thunderstorm on April 9th, struck the Monroe County law enforcement building in Sparta. The strike damaged some of the communication equipment inside the building.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning hit the Monroe County law enforcement building damaging some of the communication equipment inside the building.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A 95-year-old woman wandered away from her home on the evening of February 3rd and was found dead near railroad tracks on the morning of February 4th.  Temperatures during the period in which she was missing generally ranged from 6 to 13 degrees with wind chills as low as 5 below zero.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Temperatures fell from 13 degrees early on the evening of February 3rd, to 6 degrees during the morning of February 4th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A narrow band of heavy snow fell over northeast Wisconsin as a strengthening low pressure system moved from central Lake Michigan to northern Lake Huron.  Snow totals in the band were mainly in the 6 to 8 inch range.  Some of the highest totals included: 8.2 inches near Florence (Florence Co.), 7.0 inches at Crandon (Forest Co.), and 6.0 inches at Antigo (Langlade Co.) and Phelps (Vilas Co.).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The highest snowfall totals in Forest County were 7.0 inches at Crandon and 6.8 inches near Laona.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An April 13th wildfire near Necedah (Juneau County) burned over 300 acres before being extinguished by the Wisconsin DNR and four local fire departments. The fire was likely started by a passing train and then spread quickly by winds that gusted close to 30 mph. Flames from the fire approached a factory, which was evacuated as a precaution. The smoke spread over a residential neighborhood prompting officials to evacuate about 44 homes. No structures were burned, but one fire fighter was treated for smoke inhalation.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wildfire started by a passing train, burned 336 acres near Necedah. At one point, the flames approached a factory which was evacuated as a precaution. The smoke from the fire spread over a residential neighborhood, prompting officials to evacuate homes in the area. One fire fighter was treated for smoke inhalation. State Highway 80 south of Necedah was closed due to the fire.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rounds of thunderstorms moving east along a stationary front produced several inches of rainfall and resultant flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Four roads washed out south of Browntown, including portions of Fox Valley Road and Clark Road. Three of the roads were gravel and one was paved. Rainfall estimates of 4-5 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rounds of thunderstorms moving east along a stationary front produced several inches of rainfall and resultant flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few roads closed to due water flowing over them. A car got stuck in deep water when attempting to cross a low water crossing.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure intensified as it moved from Iowa to central Wisconsin. A surge of warm and moist air generated a large band of showers and isolated thunderstorms that moved north through southern and central Wisconsin. A few occurrences of thunderstorm wind damage from downburst winds were reported. A very brief and weak tornado was also observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree branches snapped and a shed blown over near the inlet of Lake Puckaway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A narrow band of 6 to nearly 10 inches of snow fell across far north central Wisconsin as a low pressure system moved across Illinois.  The highest snowfall totals included 9.9 inches near Argonne (Forest Co.), 9.0 inches at Three Lakes (Oneida Co.), 8.5 inches at Sugar Camp (Oneida Co.), and 8.0 inches at St. Germain and Lac du Flambeau (both in Vilas Co.).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: As much as 8.0 inches of snow fell at St. Germain and Lac du Flambeau in Vilas County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Parts of southwest Wisconsin received heavy snow to start the month of February. The area was set up for heavy snow after a cold front moved through the region on January 30th.  Heavy snow started falling during the evening of January 31st and continued into February with two day totals of 6 to 10 inches. The highest reported total was 10.5 inches near Eastman (Crawford County). In addition to the snow, winds between 20 to 40 mph caused considerable drifting.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: COOP and volunteer snow observers reported 8 to 10 inches of snow across Crawford County. The highest reported total was 10.5 inches near Eastman. North winds of 20 to 30 mph produced considerable drifting of snow across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surge of relatively mild and moist air brought a mixture of snow, sleet, and freezing drizzle to southeast WI and far southern WI. The snow and sleet fell during morning rush hour and was followed by freezing drizzle later in the morning. This resulted in snow covered roads and numerous vehicle slide-offs and accidents. The highway system in Milwaukee experienced 88 vehicle slide-offs and accidents. The ice damaged We Energies equipment causing service interruptions in Milwaukee. A Cudahy middle school closed due to the power outage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two inches of snow accumulation. Very light sleet and freezing drizzle.

Thunderstorms moved into western Wisconsin during the evening hours of May 3rd. These storms produced some hail and sub-severe wind gusts as they moved across the area. The largest hail reported was penny sized near Tomah (Monroe County).

An early morning squall line moved across southwest Wisconsin on June 22nd. This squall line produced winds of 60 to 70 mph and two small tornadoes. The first tornado damaged trees along its path north of Wauzeka (Crawford County). The second tornado damaged a few homes and numerous trees along its path north of Westport and Port Andrews (Crawford & Richland Counties). As the squall line moved across southwest Wisconsin it produced wind damage across the southern sections of Crawford and Richland Counties and over much of Grant County. Trees, power lines and at least one barn were blown down near Casseville (Grant County), multiple power lines were snapped east of Prairie du Chien (Crawford County), barns were damaged north of Port Andrew (Richland County) and 65 to 70 mph winds with damage were reported in Hazel Green and Platteville (Grant County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado traveled across rural portions of southwest Richland County north of Westport and Port Andrews. A few homes along County Roads W and M were damaged along with numerous trees.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous power lines were blown down north of Ellenboro.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Large hail fell over portions of western Wisconsin during the late afternoon and evening of June 29th. The storms started dropping hail along the Buffalo/Trempealeau County line west of Independence while moving south. The storms then exited into Minnesota before coming back into Wisconsin near Prairie du Chien (Crawford County) and then continued into Grant County. The largest hail reported was golf ball sized in Arcadia and Centerville (Trempealeau County). The hail damaged about 30 percent of the apple crop at one orchard near Centerville.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter sized hail fell just north of Trempealeau.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed along a warm front during the morning hours of October 21st over southwest Wisconsin. Some of these storms dropped nickel sized hail near Fennimore (Grant County).

Another round of severe thunderstorms with large hail and damaging straight-line winds affected parts of central and northeast Wisconsin during the afternoon of August 2nd.  The most intense storms developed near a surface boundary that stretched from Wausau to Green Bay early in the afternoon.||Some of the first severe storms of the event developed just north of Green Bay, over parts of Oconto County and Marinette County, and produced hail in excess of three inches in diameter.  These storms were part of a larger line of storms that raced eastward across the waters of Green Bay, causing widespread wind damage and knocking out power across much of Door County.||Additional storms developed westward toward the Wausau area.  These storms moved southeast during the late afternoon and produced large hail and damaging winds from Marathon and Wood counties east to Lake Michigan.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm dropped golf ball size hail as it passed through the Stevens Point area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms that developed ahead of an approaching surface boundary moved south across Wisconsin.  Some of the storms produced heavy rainfall that caused minor urban flooding, damaging winds that downed trees and power lines, and hail up to golf ball size across eastern Wisconsin.  As the storms moved across southern Lake Winnebago they produced gusts to around 40 mph that caused large waves to quickly develop, capsizing a boat with four people on board.  Three of the boat's passengers reached shore while the fourth had to be rescued by the Winnebago County Sheriff's Department.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed trees in Freedom.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another round of severe thunderstorms with large hail and damaging straight-line winds affected parts of central and northeast Wisconsin during the afternoon of August 2nd.  The most intense storms developed near a surface boundary that stretched from Wausau to Green Bay early in the afternoon.||Some of the first severe storms of the event developed just north of Green Bay, over parts of Oconto County and Marinette County, and produced hail in excess of three inches in diameter.  These storms were part of a larger line of storms that raced eastward across the waters of Green Bay, causing widespread wind damage and knocking out power across much of Door County.||Additional storms developed westward toward the Wausau area.  These storms moved southeast during the late afternoon and produced large hail and damaging winds from Marathon and Wood counties east to Lake Michigan.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm produced golf ball size hail as it passed through Auroraville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure intensified as it moved from Iowa to central Wisconsin. A surge of warm and moist air generated a large band of showers and isolated thunderstorms that moved north through southern and central Wisconsin. A few occurrences of thunderstorm wind damage from downburst winds were reported. A very brief and weak tornado was also observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Boats and docks damaged and one dead tree knocked over at Whitewater Lake.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed in a very moist air mass ahead of a warm front that was moving north into central Wisconsin.  The storms produced torrential rainfall in eastern Portage County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter in Amherst measured more than 3 inches of rain since early morning, with more than 2.75 inches falling during the evening.  The torrential rainfall caused a steet in Amherst to become impassable.

These storms were part of a larger line of storms that raced eastward across the waters of Green Bay, causing widespread wind damage and knocking out power across much of Door County.||Additional storms developed westward toward the Wausau area.  These storms moved southeast during the late afternoon and produced large hail and damaging winds from Marathon and Wood counties east to Lake Michigan.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed several trees near Peshtigo.  The time of this event is an estimate based on radar data.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe squall line moved south through eastern WI in conjunction with the passage of  a trough of low pressure. Damaging straight line winds and large hail occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees down and some powerlines down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed along a weak stationary front over far southern WI. One report of nickel size hail was received.

Thunderstorms that developed ahead of an approaching surface boundary moved south across Wisconsin.  Some of the storms produced heavy rainfall that caused minor urban flooding, damaging winds that downed trees and power lines, and hail up to golf ball size across eastern Wisconsin.  As the storms moved across southern Lake Winnebago they produced gusts to around 40 mph that caused large waves to quickly develop, capsizing a boat with four people on board.  Three of the boat's passengers reached shore while the fourth had to be rescued by the Winnebago County Sheriff's Department.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed trees and power lines on the west side of Green Bay.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The approach of a warm front brought numerous showers and thunderstorms, and heavy rain to southern WI the night of April 8th into the morning of April 9th. Low pressure then tracked along the warm front that was located along the WI and IL border in the evening. Numerous thunderstorms developed over southern WI and northern IL from the late afternoon and evening hours. Several of the thunderstorms produced severe hail in southern WI.

A strong surge of warm, moist, and unstable air ahead of an approaching warm front led to a large bow echo with damaging straight line winds across southern WI. Numerous tree damage and some structural damage occurred. A wake low with wind gusts up to 40 mph immediately followed the thunderstorms. There was no known tree damage from the wake low.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree snapped and fell on Muskego Dam Road.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three pole barns and one small barn all located southwest of Belmont suffered severe damage with the lost of the roofs and some walls collapsed. Large trees uprooted and snapped in Belmont. Trees down on one house causing severe roof damage and a couple mobile homes suffering minor damage from fallen trees or the high winds. Scattered trees and power lines were reported down throughout the remainder of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe squall line moved south through eastern WI in conjunction with the passage of  a trough of low pressure. Damaging straight line winds and large hail occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A flag pole fell on an individual, who suffered a broken arm, at the Whistling Straits golf course during the PGA Championship.  The leaderboard was also blown over.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms produced very large hail and some damaging straight line winds as they moved across far eastern Wisconsin with the passage of a cold front.
Severe thunderstorms produced very large hail and some damaging straight line winds as they moved across far eastern Wisconsin with the passage of a cold front.

Severe thunderstorms moved across portions of western Wisconsin during the afternoon and early evening of July 13th. A small, short-lived tornado developed from one of these storms as it tracked across Lone Rock (Richland County). One house sustained roof damage and had two large trees blown down onto it. Other trees were snapped off about 20 feet above the ground. The storms also dropped large hail through the afternoon and evening, generally north of Interstate 90. The largest hail reported was tennis ball sized near Osseo (Trempealeau County) where the hail cracked the sky lights on a home. Damaging winds occurred with the storms across southwest Wisconsin with trees and power lines blown down in Richland Center (Richland County) and Lone Rock.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down onto a house just outside of Richland Center.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees and powerlines were blown down across Richland Center. Two houses were damaged when trees fell on them.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of severe thunderstorms moved across western Wisconsin during the early morning hours of July 18th. The storms primarily produced damaging winds from Buffalo County into portions of Jackson and Monroe Counties. A tree was blown down onto a car in the town of Buffalo (Buffalo County) and the power was knocked out in Eleva (Trempealeau County), Black River Falls (Jackson County) and Sparta (Monroe County). Additional thunderstorms developed during the late morning of the 18th that produced a funnel cloud near Platteville (Grant County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down in Arcadia.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system moved across the Upper Mississippi River Valley and Great Lakes region. A large area of showers and thunderstorms moved across southern WI and produced 3 tornadoes in southeast WI.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF0 tornado caused sporadic tree damage along its path.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface front interacting with a warm and moist air mass, aided by an upper level disturbance, led to the development of thunderstorms.  The storms produced isolated wind damage, torrential rain, and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail fell west of Peshtigo, in the Town of Grover.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface front interacting with a warm and moist air mass, aided by an upper level disturbance, led to the development of thunderstorms.  The storms produced isolated wind damage, torrential rain, and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed a couple of trees on Highway 70 between Tipler and Florence.  Torrential rainfall accompanied the highs winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Storms, with copious moisture, ahead of a slow-moving cold front produced heavy rain as they tracked across central Wisconsin during the overnight hours of 6th and early morning hours of the 7th.  Rainfall totals in much of central Wisconsin were in the range of 2.50 to 5.00 inches.  The highest reported rainfall totals included:  5.10 inches near Junction City (Portage Co.);  4.95 inches at Marshfield Municipal Airport (Wood Co.);  4.00 inches at Nekoosa (Wood Co.);  3.85 inches, 3.81 inches and 3.53 inches at a few locations in and around Wausau (Marathon Co.);  3.84 inches WNW of New Rome (Wood Co.); 3.65 inches near Hewitt (Wood Co.); 3.35 inches near Antigo (Langlade Co.);  and 3.30 inches northeast of Stevens Point near Polonia (Portage Co.).||The heavy rain caused minor flooding, impacting mainly agricultural land, for a few hours on the Big Eau Pleine River near Stratford.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A total of 4.00 inches of rain fell at Nekoosa during the evening of September 6th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain developed as low pressure moved across Wisconsin.  The heaviest rain fell during the morning, with numerous locations in the Fox Valley receiving more than 2 inches of rain, much of which fell in just 2 to 3 hours.  The highest rainfall totals were 3.50 inches in Allouez  (Brown Co.) and 3.15 inches near Kaukauna  (Outagamie Co.).  Green Bay received 2.50 inches of rain, including 0.81 inch in just 15 minutes, more than doubling the previous record rainfall (1.21 inches in 2006) for September 8th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Torrential rainfall on the west side of Green Bay flooded roads and caused manhole covers to pop off of their bases.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the evening of September 6th, thunderstorms developed north of a nearly stationary front across the west central portions of Wisconsin. The first round of thunderstorms produced some locally heavy rains and damaging winds. A tree was blown down onto a house in Merrillan (Jackson County) and another house near Warrens (Monroe County) sustained roof and siding damage from the winds. A second round of thunderstorms then developed and moved very slowly over the same area as the previous storms. With already wet soils, the heavy rain from this second round of storms produced flash flooding across the very northern sections of Trempealeau County into northern Jackson County and southern Clark County. In Eleva (Trempealeau County), flood waters covered State Highway 93 and U.S. Highway 10 and caused around $400 thousand in damage to a food business. Numerous homes and businesses in Osseo sustained water damage from flooding along the North and South Forks of the Buffalo River. The flood waters covered or washed out numerous roads in Jackson and Clark Counties. Two accidents occurred in Jackson County when people tried to drive their vehicles over water covered roads that had been washed out. The dam at Emerson Lake (Clark County) was damaged by the flood waters with repairs estimated at almost a quarter of a million dollars. State of Emergencies were declared in Jackson and Trempealeau Counties. Total damages were estimated at $1.8 million in Trempealeau County, $800 thousand in Clark County and $400 thousand in Jackson County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down and damage to a roof and siding occurred in Warrens.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flood waters from the Black River covered County Road G just west of Greenwood. Waters flowing through a drainage ditch undercut Chickadee Road near Gorman Avenue southwest of Willard. County Highway M was closed north of County Highway I when a culvert was washed out. This is the second time in three years that this section of County M has been washed out. County Highway B was undermined by water west of Humbird. The flood waters also prompted the closing of the county's ATV and horse trails. The flood waters damaged the dam at Emerson Lake with repairs estimated to cost almost a quarter of a million dollars. Highway damage along was estimated at $536,200.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flood waters from Adams Creek went over State Highway 93 just north of Eleva and U.S. Highway 10 on the east side of Eleva. One business and one home suffered major damage from the flood waters. The business, a food wholesaler, suffered damage to a food storage building, had around 30% of their frozen food inventory destroyed and several diesel trucks were damaged causing an estimated $400 thousand in damages. The home suffered major damage when the basement completely filled with water and the main floor was also covered with water. Six other businesses in Eleva sustained minor water damage. The flood waters along the Buffalo River caused the Buffalo River State Trail to be shut down west of Eleva into Buffalo County.

The heavy rain also flooded numerous basements throughout town with about 40 structures sustaining minor water damage. The flood waters along the Buffalo River caused the Buffalo River State Trail to be shut down from west of Osseo into Jackson County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the evening of September 6th, thunderstorms developed north of a nearly stationary front across the west central portions of Wisconsin. The first round of thunderstorms produced some locally heavy rains and damaging winds. A tree was blown down onto a house in Merrillan (Jackson County) and another house near Warrens (Monroe County) sustained roof and siding damage from the winds. A second round of thunderstorms then developed and moved very slowly over the same area as the previous storms. With already wet soils, the heavy rain from this second round of storms produced flash flooding across the very northern sections of Trempealeau County into northern Jackson County and southern Clark County. In Eleva (Trempealeau County), flood waters covered State Highway 93 and U.S. Highway 10 and caused around $400 thousand in damage to a food business. Numerous homes and businesses in Osseo sustained water damage from flooding along the North and South Forks of the Buffalo River. The flood waters covered or washed out numerous roads in Jackson and Clark Counties. Two accidents occurred in Jackson County when people tried to drive their vehicles over water covered roads that had been washed out. The dam at Emerson Lake (Clark County) was damaged by the flood waters with repairs estimated at almost a quarter of a million dollars. State of Emergencies were declared in Jackson and Trempealeau Counties. Total damages were estimated at $1.8 million in Trempealeau County, $800 thousand in Clark County and $400 thousand in Jackson County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: East of Loyal, 4.75 inches of rain fell.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Storms, with copious moisture, ahead of a slow-moving cold front produced heavy rain as they tracked across central Wisconsin during the overnight hours of 6th and early morning hours of the 7th.  Rainfall totals in much of central Wisconsin were in the range of 2.50 to 5.00 inches.  The highest reported rainfall totals included:  5.10 inches near Junction City (Portage Co.);  4.95 inches at Marshfield Municipal Airport (Wood Co.);  4.00 inches at Nekoosa (Wood Co.);  3.85 inches, 3.81 inches and 3.53 inches at a few locations in and around Wausau (Marathon Co.);  3.84 inches WNW of New Rome (Wood Co.); 3.65 inches near Hewitt (Wood Co.); 3.35 inches near Antigo (Langlade Co.);  and 3.30 inches northeast of Stevens Point near Polonia (Portage Co.).||The heavy rain caused minor flooding, impacting mainly agricultural land, for a few hours on the Big Eau Pleine River near Stratford.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused flooding of CR-F about 5 miles east of Colby.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system brought heavy rains to western Wisconsin in the middle of December. Temperatures with this system remained above freezing allowing all the precipitation to fall as rain. Amounts ranged from around 2 inches to over 6 inches from Jackson County south to Grant County. The highest reported rainfall total in this portion of western Wisconsin was 6.27 inches near Viroqua (Vernon County). This rain caused in bank rises to occur on most rivers and streams along with minor river flooding along the Black, Yellow, Wisconsin and Kickapoo Rivers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from heavy rains pushed the Kickapoo River out of its banks in Gays Mills. The river crested at 14.65 feet, which was around 1.5 feet over the flood stage.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: More than two inches of rain fell in a little more than 90 minutes in Marshfield on ground that was saturated by earlier rainfall.  The rain caused street and basement flooding in Marshfield, and filled ditches that overflowed onto roads in nearby rural areas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several waves of thunderstorms moved across east central Minnesota, and into west central Wisconsin during the morning of Thursday, September, 17th. These storms dropped up to 5-6 inches of rain in Barron County before 9 AM LST. There were reports of washed out roads near Cameron, Barron and Chetek. Heavy rainfall, and associated runoff, also caused several creeks near Bloomer in Chippewa County to flood and caused one highway west of Bloomer to close.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Excessive rainfall amounts of 3 to 6 inches occurred in parts of Polk County which led to roads washed out and closed. Two of the roads were near Clayton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several waves of thunderstorms moved across east central Minnesota, and into west central Wisconsin during the morning of Thursday, September, 17th. These storms dropped up to 5-6 inches of rain in Barron County before 9 AM LST. There were reports of washed out roads near Cameron, Barron and Chetek. Heavy rainfall, and associated runoff, also caused several creeks near Bloomer in Chippewa County to flood and caused one highway west of Bloomer to close.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several reports on media and on social media had significant flooding along the Como Creek, near Bloomer. It closed parts of Highway 40, along the creek, west of Bloomer for several hours in the morning. In addition, a social media picture had street flooding on Duncan Creek in the town of Bloomer. The flood waters also had covered up Bloomer High School practice football field, a baseball field and the old playground for elementary school.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A few thunderstorms developed along a a slow moving cold front near the WI and IL border. One large hail report was received.

A rural Black River Falls (Jackson County) house was damaged when it was hit by a lightning strike during the evening of September 17th. The lightning struck the house's TV antenna and traveled into the house blowing out a section of an interior and exterior wall and causing damage to the electrical wiring in the house.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning hit a rural Black River Falls home striking the house's TV antenna and then traveling into the house. A hole was blown into an interior wall of a second floor bedroom where a mattress and box springs caught on fire. A wall stud was blown apart causing a hole in an exterior wall and numerous outlets and light switches throughout the house had their covers blown off or charred.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface front interacting with a warm and moist air mass, aided by an upper level disturbance, led to the development of thunderstorms.  The storms produced isolated wind damage, torrential rain, and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail was found on the ground east of Tipler about thirty minutes after a severe thunderstorm passed through that area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A few thunderstorms developed along a a slow moving cold front near the WI and IL border. One large hail report was received.

A surge of warm, moist, unstable air ahead of an approaching cold front resulted in rounds of numerous thunderstorms across southern WI. A few thunderstorms became severe with damaging wind gusts. Heavy rainfall also occurred across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A swath of wind damage with numerous branches and trees down including one large uprooted tree. Trees and branches down on cars and one house. Power poles and powerlines down. Street signs blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surge of warm, moist, unstable air ahead of an approaching cold front resulted in rounds of numerous thunderstorms across southern WI. A few thunderstorms became severe with damaging wind gusts. Heavy rainfall also occurred across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One large tree uprooted and some shingles removed from the roof of a home.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surge of warm, moist, unstable air ahead of an approaching cold front resulted in rounds of numerous thunderstorms across southern WI. A few thunderstorms became severe with damaging wind gusts. Heavy rainfall also occurred across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Urban and rural street flooding occurred across the county due to 3 to 4 inches of rainfall over several hours. No road closures were needed and the water did subside during the early morning hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Previous thunderstorms that moved across western Wisconsin during the morning hours of Wednesday, September 2nd, provided a boundary that fired off additional thunderstorms in west central Wisconsin during the early afternoon. These storms continued to develop in a small area that reformed west of Ridgeland in far northwest Dunn County, and drifted east-southeast across northern Dunn and western Chippewa Counties.  Rainfall amounts averaged between 3-5 inches across the flooded areas, with locally 6-8 inches near Ridgeland where locals indicated that they hadn't seen this type of flooding in years. One individual could not get out of his driveway due to a flooded creek that ran along his property line. The village of Ridgeland, which is only a few blocks long, had nearly all their roads closed during the height of the flooding. ||Flooding was reported on Highway 25, County Roads W and WW near Ridgeland. Several of the roads were damaged around Ridgeland and south to southeast toward the Dunn/Chippewa County line. | |In Chippewa County, County Highway S was closed east of the U.S. Highway 53 due to a washout.  ||Other roads that were impassable due to washouts in both Dunn and Chippewa Counties:| |Highway F south of Highway N|Highway T & Highway N intersection|Highway Q at 129th St.|Highway S, East of Highway HH|Highway C and State Highway 40||Although most of the heavy rainfall ended by 2 PM CST, runoff continued through the late afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Local county officials, trained observers and the general public indicated widespread flooding across portions of northern Dunn County. Numerous roads were either closed or partially closed during the height of the flood event. The village of Ridgeland was inundated with flood waters, and some roads were impassable. Rainfall reports in the area had averaged between 4 to 6 inches, with locally 8 inches near Ridgeland. ||Specifically, flooding was reported on Highway 25, County Roads W and WW near Ridgeland. Other parts of Dunn County, including Highway Q at 129th St, and Highway S, east of Highway HH, were closed. ||Several of the roads were damaged around Ridgeland and south to southeast toward the Dunn/Chippewa County line.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Previous thunderstorms that moved across western Wisconsin during the morning hours of Wednesday, September 2nd, provided a boundary that fired off additional thunderstorms in west central Wisconsin during the early afternoon. These storms continued to develop in a small area that reformed west of Ridgeland in far northwest Dunn County, and drifted east-southeast across northern Dunn and western Chippewa Counties.  Rainfall amounts averaged between 3-5 inches across the flooded areas, with locally 6-8 inches near Ridgeland where locals indicated that they hadn't seen this type of flooding in years. One individual could not get out of his driveway due to a flooded creek that ran along his property line. The village of Ridgeland, which is only a few blocks long, had nearly all their roads closed during the height of the flooding. ||Flooding was reported on Highway 25, County Roads W and WW near Ridgeland. Several of the roads were damaged around Ridgeland and south to southeast toward the Dunn/Chippewa County line. | |In Chippewa County, County Highway S was closed east of the U.S. Highway 53 due to a washout.  ||Other roads that were impassable due to washouts in both Dunn and Chippewa Counties:| |Highway F south of Highway N|Highway T & Highway N intersection|Highway Q at 129th St.|Highway S, East of Highway HH|Highway C and State Highway 40||Although most of the heavy rainfall ended by 2 PM CST, runoff continued through the late afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Local county officials, trained observers, and the general public indicated widespread flooding across portions of northwest and western Chippewa County as locally 4 to 6 inches of rain fell in less than 3 hours. Several roads were closed during the height of the storm, plus the city of Chippewa Falls had some problems with flooded roadways. In addition, five feet of water was reported in the basement of one residence in Chippewa Falls and at St. Joseph's Hospital in Chippewa Falls. In addition, County Highway S was closed east of the U.S. Highway 53 due to a washout. Other roads in Chippewa county, including Highway C and State Highway 40, and Highway T at the Highway N intersection, were closed due to flood waters.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed along a cold front that moved across west central Wisconsin during the evening of Sunday, September 6th. Several thunderstorm complexes moved across southern Eau Claire County and produced up to 3 to 5 inches in a 3 hour period. Creeks were overflowing near Augusta while several other roads were inundated with flood waters across the southern one-quarter of the county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Local officials reported several roads closed across southern Eau Claire County due to excessive rainfall, with amounts of 3 to 5 inches in a 3 hour period. Several culverts were damaged along Highway 53 and Highway 12 near Augusta. State officials estimated that there was about $300,000 in road damage in Eau Claire County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A band of thunderstorms moved across portions of Pepin and southern Eau Claire Counties during the morning of Tuesday, September 8th. Although the rainfall was not particularly heavy Tuesday morning, Sunday's rainfall with amounts of 3 to 5 inches had occurred in the same area. Therefore, only a short period of heavy rainfall caused a few roads near Allen and Clear Creek to be flooded.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flood waters covered the intersection of Route D and Route HH near Clear Creek.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several waves of thunderstorms moved across east central Minnesota, and into west central Wisconsin during the morning of Thursday, September, 17th. These storms dropped up to 5-6 inches of rain in Barron County before 9 AM LST. There were reports of washed out roads near Cameron, Barron and Chetek. Heavy rainfall, and associated runoff, also caused several creeks near Bloomer in Chippewa County to flood and caused one highway west of Bloomer to close.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Parts of 9th Avenue between 5th and County Road P, near Arland, were washed out due to excessive rainfall. Significant street flooding was noted at 15th Avenue and west of 10th Avenue near Poskin, along the Vermillion River. In addition, there were reports of water over the road north of Barron, near the Yellow River.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Kenosha County Medical Examiner reported a death due to hypothermia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Kenosha County Medical Examiner reported the death of an elderly man due to hypothermia. The temperatures were below zero during the early morning hours with high temperatures in the teens Fahrenheit.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms moved across portions of western Wisconsin during the afternoon and early evening of July 13th. A small, short-lived tornado developed from one of these storms as it tracked across Lone Rock (Richland County). One house sustained roof damage and had two large trees blown down onto it. Other trees were snapped off about 20 feet above the ground. The storms also dropped large hail through the afternoon and evening, generally north of Interstate 90. The largest hail reported was tennis ball sized near Osseo (Trempealeau County) where the hail cracked the sky lights on a home. Damaging winds occurred with the storms across southwest Wisconsin with trees and power lines blown down in Richland Center (Richland County) and Lone Rock.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees were blown down southeast of Cataract.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The evening of Sunday, July 5th, a line of thunderstorms developed along a frontal boundary from west central to northeast Minnesota. By 2 to 3 am CST, numerous thunderstorms began to develop in east central Minnesota, ahead of the main line.  As additional thunderstorms congealed with the main line, and deep moisture continued to surge toward western Wisconsin, the added effect of high precipitable water values resulted in rainfall rates increasing to 2-3 inches per hour. These storms moved eastward into west central Wisconsin. ||Several hours of moderate to heavy rainfall occurred over St. Croix, Pierce and Dunn counties during the early morning hours of Monday, July 6th. ||Storm totals ranged from 5-8 inches in portions of St. Croix and Pierce counties,  where locally 7.31 inches was measured around River Falls. The Kinnickinnic River near River Falls rose almost 7 feet in less than 5 hours. ||The Troy Glen Trailer Park (consisting of about 20 homes) was evacuated to the Troy Town Hall, and the American Red Cross assisted in the displaced residents in Hudson. St. Croix and Dunn Counties reported some roads covered with water. Chippewa County reported the intersection of County Highway K and 170th Avenue (6 miles south of Cornell) was flooded. ||County emergency management reported damage from municipalities, to roads, a couple bridges, culverts, ditches and houses with flooded basements. The hardest hit townships were Baldwin, Cady, Eau Galle, Kinnickinnic, Rush River, St. Joseph, Troy, Wilson, Woodville and especially River Falls.  Estimated damage to the flood in St. Croix County alone was $360,000. ||The following are some of the higher rainfall totals from Monday morning, July 6th.||River Falls 7.31 inches. |St. Croix 7.10 inches. |Wheeler 4.90 inches. |Nye 4.12 inches. |Osceola 3.65 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Troy Glen Trailer Park (consisting of about 20 homes) was evacuated to the Troy Town Hall due to flood waters, and the American Red Cross assisted in the displaced residents.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex of thunderstorms developed during the evening hours of July 12th over Minnesota. As these storms move southeast during the early morning hours of the 13th, they strengthened over central Wisconsin and started producing damaging winds. A mesonet reporting station near Necedah (Juneau County) measured a 62 mph wind gust and trees were blown down in Arkdale and Petenwell County Park (Adams County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mesonet reporting station near Necedah measured a wind gust of 62 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms moved across portions of western Wisconsin during the afternoon and early evening of July 13th. A small, short-lived tornado developed from one of these storms as it tracked across Lone Rock (Richland County). One house sustained roof damage and had two large trees blown down onto it. Other trees were snapped off about 20 feet above the ground. The storms also dropped large hail through the afternoon and evening, generally north of Interstate 90. The largest hail reported was tennis ball sized near Osseo (Trempealeau County) where the hail cracked the sky lights on a home. Damaging winds occurred with the storms across southwest Wisconsin with trees and power lines blown down in Richland Center (Richland County) and Lone Rock.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A small, short-lived tornado produced roof damage and blew down two large trees onto a house in Lone Rock. Other trees were snapped off about 20 feet above the ground.

A significant bow echo moved across Minnesota and then into west central Wisconsin during the early morning hours of Saturday, July 18th. These storms moved rapidly eastward, with numerous reports of downed trees and power lines. A few intense bow echo segments produced damage to sheds and outbuildings. One of the stronger storms  produced an EF-1 tornado near Eau Galle.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree was blown down north of Weston.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A significant bow echo moved across Minnesota and then into west central Wisconsin during the early morning hours of Saturday, July 18th. These storms moved rapidly eastward, with numerous reports of downed trees and power lines. A few intense bow echo segments produced damage to sheds and outbuildings. One of the stronger storms  produced an EF-1 tornado near Eau Galle.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A storm survey indicated an EF-1 occurred near Eau Galle, Wisconsin. The EF-1 tornado formed one mile northwest of Eau Galle, and continued generally east to four miles east of Eau Galle. Most of the damage was down trees and power lines. The greatest damage occurred just north of Eau Galle, where trees were snapped and uprooted, and a home weather station reported a maximum wind gust of 88 mph. The maximum width was 50 yards and was on the ground for approximately 5.3 miles.

A winter storm hit western Wisconsin on December 28th and 29th with heavy snow, sleet and strong winds.  As the storm lifted north out of the Southern Plains states, snow moved into western Wisconsin during the late morning of the 28th. For parts of southwest Wisconsin, this snow was mixed with or completely changed over to sleet during the afternoon and early evening of the 28th. The heaviest snow fell during the late afternoon and early evening of the 28th with 1 to 2 inch per hour rates. This caused a rapid deterioration in road conditions causing travel conditions to quickly become dangerous.  By the time the snow ended on the 29th, totals across western Wisconsin ranged from 5 to 11 inches. The highest reported total was 11.5 inches near Black River Falls (Jackson County) and Warrens (Monroe County). Blowing and drifting of the snow occurred as winds gusted into the 25 to 40 mph range.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: COOP and volunteer snow observers reported 5 to 6 inches of snow across Grant County. The highest reported total was 6.5 inches in Sinsinawa. The snow mixed with or completely changed over to sleet during the afternoon and evening of the 28th. Winds gusting into the 35 to 40 mph range created considerable blowing and drifting snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms moved across portions of western Wisconsin during the afternoon and early evening of July 13th. A small, short-lived tornado developed from one of these storms as it tracked across Lone Rock (Richland County). One house sustained roof damage and had two large trees blown down onto it. Other trees were snapped off about 20 feet above the ground. The storms also dropped large hail through the afternoon and evening, generally north of Interstate 90. The largest hail reported was tennis ball sized near Osseo (Trempealeau County) where the hail cracked the sky lights on a home. Damaging winds occurred with the storms across southwest Wisconsin with trees and power lines blown down in Richland Center (Richland County) and Lone Rock.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Half dollar sized hail fell southeast of Viroqua.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The evening of Sunday, July 5th, a line of thunderstorms developed along a frontal boundary from west central to northeast Minnesota. By 2 to 3 am CST, numerous thunderstorms began to develop in east central Minnesota, ahead of the main line.  As additional thunderstorms congealed with the main line, and deep moisture continued to surge toward western Wisconsin, the added effect of high precipitable water values resulted in rainfall rates increasing to 2-3 inches per hour. These storms moved eastward into west central Wisconsin. ||Several hours of moderate to heavy rainfall occurred over St. Croix, Pierce and Dunn counties during the early morning hours of Monday, July 6th. ||Storm totals ranged from 5-8 inches in portions of St. Croix and Pierce counties,  where locally 7.31 inches was measured around River Falls. The Kinnickinnic River near River Falls rose almost 7 feet in less than 5 hours. ||The Troy Glen Trailer Park (consisting of about 20 homes) was evacuated to the Troy Town Hall, and the American Red Cross assisted in the displaced residents in Hudson. St. Croix and Dunn Counties reported some roads covered with water. Chippewa County reported the intersection of County Highway K and 170th Avenue (6 miles south of Cornell) was flooded. ||County emergency management reported damage from municipalities, to roads, a couple bridges, culverts, ditches and houses with flooded basements. The hardest hit townships were Baldwin, Cady, Eau Galle, Kinnickinnic, Rush River, St. Joseph, Troy, Wilson, Woodville and especially River Falls.  Estimated damage to the flood in St. Croix County alone was $360,000. ||The following are some of the higher rainfall totals from Monday morning, July 6th.||River Falls 7.31 inches. |St. Croix 7.10 inches. |Wheeler 4.90 inches. |Nye 4.12 inches. |Osceola 3.65 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flood waters damaged a shoulder of County Road U near Glover Road.

A complex of thunderstorms developed during the evening hours of July 12th over Minnesota. As these storms move southeast during the early morning hours of the 13th, they strengthened over central Wisconsin and started producing damaging winds. A mesonet reporting station near Necedah (Juneau County) measured a 62 mph wind gust and trees were blown down in Arkdale and Petenwell County Park (Adams County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The automated weather reporting equipment at Volk Air Field on the Camp Douglas National Guard Base measured a wind gust of 58 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong westerly wind gusts caused downed branches and power lines to southeast WI. We Energies reported up to 15000 customers were without power. The strong wind gusts mainly in the 50 mph range were caused by the passage of strong low pressure and its cold front.

A line of severe thunderstorms moved across western Wisconsin during the early morning hours of July 18th. The storms primarily produced damaging winds from Buffalo County into portions of Jackson and Monroe Counties. A tree was blown down onto a car in the town of Buffalo (Buffalo County) and the power was knocked out in Eleva (Trempealeau County), Black River Falls (Jackson County) and Sparta (Monroe County). Additional thunderstorms developed during the late morning of the 18th that produced a funnel cloud near Platteville (Grant County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down across the southbound lane of U.S. Highway 12/State Highway 27 on the south side of Black River Falls. The winds also took down a powerline in the same area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed during the late evening of August 1st over northwest and north central Wisconsin. As these storms moved east, one of them dropped large hail, up to the size of a half dollar, south of Rib Lake (Taylor County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Half dollar sized hail fell south of Rib Lake.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms that moved across central Minnesota and portions of east central Minnesota, moved rapidly across west central Wisconsin. Although there were several reports of large trees blown down and power outages from downed power lines, the worst part of the storm occurred near Stanley were some structural damage to houses occurred and power outages lasted for several hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were uprooted near Luck.

A strengthening polar trough and front brought heavy snow to southern Wisconsin, mainly along and south of a line from Lone Rock to Milwaukee. Lake enhanced snow contributed to the overall snow totals over far southeast Wisconsin. Snow totals ranged from 5 to 18 inches with the heaviest totals in the counties bordering Illinois. Wet, heavy snow brought branches down onto electrical wires causing power outages mainly in the counties bordering Illinois where the heaviest snow fell. Vehicle slide-offs and accidents were prevalent.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Six to ten inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm hit western Wisconsin on December 28th and 29th with heavy snow, sleet and strong winds.  As the storm lifted north out of the Southern Plains states, snow moved into western Wisconsin during the late morning of the 28th. For parts of southwest Wisconsin, this snow was mixed with or completely changed over to sleet during the afternoon and early evening of the 28th. The heaviest snow fell during the late afternoon and early evening of the 28th with 1 to 2 inch per hour rates. This caused a rapid deterioration in road conditions causing travel conditions to quickly become dangerous.  By the time the snow ended on the 29th, totals across western Wisconsin ranged from 5 to 11 inches. The highest reported total was 11.5 inches near Black River Falls (Jackson County) and Warrens (Monroe County). Blowing and drifting of the snow occurred as winds gusted into the 25 to 40 mph range.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: COOP and volunteer snow observers reported 8 to 11 inches of snow across Jackson County. The highest reported total was 11.5 inches near Black River Falls. Winds gusting into the 30 to 35 mph range created blowing and drifting snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system affected northwest Wisconsin on November 12th and 13th. The precipitation began as rain and gradually switched to snow. A combination of a gravity wave and lake effect snow resulted in pockets of up to 10 to 14 in Iron and Bayfield counties. Snowfall reports included 13 in Iron River, 14 near Cornucopia, and 12 in Gile.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure tracked across the Central Plains and spread a band of heavy snow into southwest Wisconsin during the afternoon of November 20th. The snow continued through much of the early morning hours of the 21st before tapering off during the late morning. The heaviest snow fell primarily south of U.S. Highway 18 with amounts of 6 to 10 inches common and there were some localized amounts in excess of a foot. The highest reported totals were across Grant County with 14 inches southeast of Platteville and 12 inches near Lancaster and in Platteville.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: COOP and volunteer observers measured between 4 and 7 inches of snow across Crawford County. The highest reported total was 7.1 inches in Prairie du Chien.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A power pole was blown down in the village of Belgium. A 10 inch diameter tree was blown down and blocking a roadway near Saukville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strengthening polar trough and front brought heavy snow to southern Wisconsin, mainly along and south of a line from Lone Rock to Milwaukee. Lake enhanced snow contributed to the overall snow totals over far southeast Wisconsin. Snow totals ranged from 5 to 18 inches with the heaviest totals in the counties bordering Illinois. Wet, heavy snow brought branches down onto electrical wires causing power outages mainly in the counties bordering Illinois where the heaviest snow fell. Vehicle slide-offs and accidents were prevalent.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Eleven to sixteen inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very strong low pressure system moved across the region during the early morning hours of December 24th. Widespread winds of 35 to 45 mph affected central, north central, and east central Wisconsin. The strong winds blew down some trees, numerous tree branches were knocked down and thousands lost power.  Some of the strongest wind gusts included:  59 mph near Whitefish Dunes State Park (Door Co.), 52 mph at Algoma (Kewaunee Co.), 51 mph in Green Bay (Brown Co.), and 46 mph near Appleton (Outagamie Co.).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A gust to 47 mph was measured at the airport in Oconto during the early morning hours of December 24.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The first major winter storm of the 2015-2016 winter season for the region produced a swath of 8 to 14 inches of snow across parts of central and northeast Wisconsin as a low pressure system moved from Arkansas to southern Lake Michigan.  Much of the snow fell in just eight hours.  In addition to the snow, sleet fell in parts of east-central Wisconsin and thundersnow was reported at several locations, including Green Bay and the Lake Michigan shoreline.  The heavy, wet snow caused numerous vehicles to become stranded across Green Bay and the Fox Valley.  Two unoccupied vehicles that were stuck in the snow on railroad tracks were later hit by trains (one in the Green Bay area and the other in Appleton).||Winds were very gusty across the area, mostly 35 to 45 mph, causing blowing and drifting of the newly fallen snow that created blizzard conditions in Brown County and Outagamie County. The highest reported gusts included 61 mph at Algoma (Kewaunee Co.), 54 mph at Green Bay (Brown Co.) and 53 mph near Marinette (Marinette Co.).||Some of the heaviest snow accumulations included: 14.6 in New London (Waupaca Co.); 13.5 near Two Rivers (Manitowoc Co.), in De Pere (Brown Co.), and near Ephraim (Door Co.); and 13.2 inches in Green Bay (Brown Co.).  The storm total in Green Bay made it the fourth greatest December snowstorm on record for the city.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snowfall in Stevens Point was 8.9 inches, and winds gusted to 44 mph at Plover.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong winter storm, but with mild temperatures, moved across the Upper Midwest during the day of Wednesday, December 23rd. Bands of mainly light snow developed early Wednesday morning, intensified during the afternoon, before tapering off Wednesday evening. Some light rain and drizzle also occurred as warmer air wrapped into this system during the day. This warmer air limited snowfall amounts in west central Wisconsin where most areas only received between 2 and 4 inches of snow. However, there was a small area in northern Polk, and northern Barron counties that received locally six inches of snow in a 12 hour period. The heaviest snow fell in far northern Wisconsin where locally 6 to 10 inches fell.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few areas in far northern Barron County, near Cumberland and Barronett received locally 6 inches of snow. Some light rain and light drizzle also developed during the afternoon and compacted the snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm affected Southern WI as strong low pressure tracked from the Lower Mississippi River Valley to Northeast IL and Lower MI. Most areas saw 5 to 10 inches of wet snow and sleet combined, with sleet accumulations up to two inches in some areas. East to northeast wind gusts of 30 to 45 mph occurred with visibility restrictions of 1/4 to 1/2 mile. There were more than 300 accidents on interstate and state highways mainly in the Milwaukee and Madison areas. Two men collapsed and died while shoveling the afternoon of Dec. 29th in Milwaukee County. A Golf Dome was damaged by the weight of the heavy snow in Milwaukee County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wet snow and sleet accumulation of 5 to 10 inches. East to northeast wind gusts up to 45 mph. Visibility reduced to 1/4 to 1/2 mile at times.
